elements.sent-2411-001: The SSS RX~J$0513.9-6951$ in the Large Magellanic Cloud~(LMC) exhibits cyclic variations between optical low and high states, which are anti-correlated with its X-ray flux.
elements.sent-2411-002: This behaviour is believed to result from the periodic expansion and contraction of the~WD due to variations in the accretion rate in the system.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our aim is to test a contraction model of the source variability by tracking the evolution of the WD properties.
elements.sent-2411-004: The LMC~chemical composition of the atmospheres was assumed.
elements.sent-2411-005: This evolution follows the model tracks of WDs with masses of $1.05-1.15\,M_{\sun}$ and thermonuclear burning on the surface.
elements.sent-2411-006: We show that, when the source has a relatively small photospheric radius and is optically bright, it lies below the stable-burning strip with a relatively low bolometric luminosity.
elements.sent-2411-007: Conversely, the fainter optical states correspond to higher bolometric luminosity and larger photospheric radii of the hot~WD.
elements.sent-2411-008: RXJ0513 lies within the stable-burning strip during this state.
elements.sent-2411-009: This means that the optical brightness of the system is lower when the~WD is larger, more luminous, and more effectively illuminates the accretion disc.
elements.sent-2411-010: These results contradict the contraction model, which predicts the opposite behaviour of the source.
elements.sent-2411-011: We use a model, that assumes that the far UV/soft X-ray flux is reprocessed into the optical band due to multiple scattering in the cloud system above the accretion disc.
elements.sent-2411-012: More significant illumination can lead to rarefying of the cloud slab, thereby reducing the reprocessing efficiency and making the source fainter in the optical band.
elements.sent-2411-013: Supersoft X-ray sources~(SSS) represent a subclass of cataclysmic variable~(CV) stars characterised by a high mass-accretion rate of~${\sim 10^{-7}\,M_\odot\, \rm yr^{-1}$ and (quasi-)steady state thermonuclear burning on the white dwarf~(WD) surface~\citep{van_den_Heuvel_etal1992, Rappaport_etal1994 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: CVs are close binary systems composed of a WD primary and a main-sequence or red (sub-)giant companion that fills its Roche lobe.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, matter transfers from the companion to the~WD, potentially forming an accretion disc.
elements.sent-2411-016: The first~SSSs were identified in the Magellanic Clouds by the Einstein observatory~\citep{Long_etal1981, Seward_Mitchell1981 , and they were later classified as a new type of X-ray source during ROSAT~observations~\citep{Trumper_etal1991 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: These sources are defined by their extremely soft thermal X-ray spectra, with blackbody temperatures ranging from $15-80$~eV ($150-900\,\rm kK$) and high luminosities of~${\sim 10^{36 -10^{38 \rm\, erg/s$, which are comparable to the Eddington luminosity of a solar mass object~\citep{Kahabka_van_den_Heuvel1997, Greiner2000 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: To date, more than $100$~SSSs have been identified in approximately $20$~external galaxies, the Magellanic Clouds, and our Galaxy~\citep{Greiner2000, Kahabka2006, Maitra_etal2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The luminous transient soft X-ray source RX~J$0513.9-6951$~(hereafter RXJ0513) in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) was discovered in the ROSAT All-Sky Survey~\citep{Schaeidt_etal1993, Pakull_etal1993, Cowley_etal1993 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The optical spectroscopic analysis revealed strong emission lines of \element{H , \ion{He {II and several higher ionisation emission features with broad wings, indicating the presence of an accretion disc, while the accretor is most likely a~WD~\citep{Crampton_etal1996, Southwell_etal1996 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Thanks to the Massive Compact Halo Objects project (MACHO) survey~\citep{Alcock_etal1996 , a~$3.5$~yr light curve of the V ${\sim}\, 17^m$ optical counterpart of this source was obtained, which revealed recurrent low states~(dropping by ${\sim}\, 1^m$), which last $20-40$~days and repeat every $100-200$ days.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, these optical low states are accompanied by X-ray outbursts, so the optical and X-ray states are strictly anti-correlated~\citep{Southwell_etal1996 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Such variability is explained by the so-called contraction model~\citep[e.g.][]{Reinsch_etal1996, Reinsch_etal2000, Southwell_etal1996, Hachisu_etal2003a, Hachisu_etal2003b , which suggests that the observed changes occur due to a change in the mass accretion rate, and therefore, a change in the intensity of thermonuclear burning on the WD~surface.}
elements.sent-2411-024: High rate of thermonuclear energy release leads to a significant expansion of a WD~photosphere, and the peak in emergent radiation lies outside the observed X-ray band, which makes the source faint in X-ray.
elements.sent-2411-025: At the same time the bolometric luminosity of the expanded~WD is high, and it effectively illuminates the accretion disc, resulting in high optical flux due to the reprocessing of the WD~radiation.
elements.sent-2411-026: This scenario corresponds to the optically bright and X-ray faint state.
elements.sent-2411-027: On the other hand, a low mass accretion rate causes the~WD to shrink, and its outgoing radiation is observed in the soft X-ray band.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, a smaller photospheric radius and decreased WD~luminosity make the accretion disc illumination less significant, resulting in faint optical brightness.
elements.sent-2411-029: The origin of the periodicity in the mass accretion rate changes is not completely clear.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this model, the mass transfer from the companion star to the WD remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-031: Alternatively, \citet{Hachisu_etal2003a, Hachisu_etal2003b suggested the `accretion wind evolution' model, in which a self-sustained optically thick wind from the~WD impacts the companion star, reducing the mass transfer rate.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This diminishes the wind, causing the mass transfer rate to increase again.
elements.sent-2411-033: The behaviour of such a wind modulates the accretion rate onto the~WD, resulting in the transitions between high and low optical states.
elements.sent-2411-034: A WD~irradiation of the companion star was proposed to be an origin of the periodic mass transfer.
elements.sent-2411-035: However, interpreting SSS~spectra presents significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-036: While low-resolution spectra can often be described by a blackbody model~\citep[as done by][]{McGowan_etal2005 , this approach lacks physical accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In general, blackbody fitting leads to an overestimation of the interstellar neutral hydrogen column density,~$\nh$, and underestimation of the temperature, resulting in an overestimated luminosity~\citep[see e.g.][]{Greiner_etal1991, Heise_etal1994 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: High-resolution spectra of SSSs are considerably more complex, featuring numerous emission and absorption lines.
elements.sent-2411-039: The spectra of~SSSs can be more accurately described by theoretical spectra of hot WD~atmospheres.
elements.sent-2411-040: This approach has been applied by numerous authors using both local thermodynamic equilibrium~\citep[LTE, e.g.][]{Heise_etal1994, Ibragimov_etal2003, Suleimanov_Igragimov2003, Burwitz_etal2007, Swartz_etal2002 , and non-LTE~\citep[e.g.][]{HartmannHeise1996, Lanz_etal2005, Petz_etal2005, vanRossum2012, Rauch_etal2010 assumptions.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Non-LTE effects are non-negligible, and non-LTE models are more physically accurate, all else being equal.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, the physical conditions of the radiating regions in~SSSs are not entirely clear and may differ significantly from the simplest plane-parallel hydrostatic atmosphere for which non-LTE effects are considered.
elements.sent-2411-043: The uncertainties associated with factors like radiation-driven winds, boundary layers, accretion disc irradiance are likely much more significant than non-LTE effects.
elements.sent-2411-044: Therefore, we believe that LTE~models provide comparable accuracy~\citep[see][]{Suleimanov_etal2024, Tavleev_etal2024 and can be used to analyse the soft X-ray spectra of~SSSs.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Moreover, LTE~computations are much faster, allowing for the calculation of more extensive model grids and the inclusion of a larger number of ions, their excited levels, and a greater number of spectral lines compared to the non-LTE case.
elements.sent-2411-046: Recently \citet{Suleimanov_etal2024 further refined the LTE~approach, including a more extensive set of spectral lines and photoionisation opacities from the excited levels of heavy element ions.}
elements.sent-2411-047: They successfully applied a computed set of model atmosphere spectra to the interpretation of grating \chandra~and XMM-Newton~spectra of two SSS~sources, CAL~$83$ and RXJ0513; for latter source only one \chandra~observation was used.
elements.sent-2411-048: It was concluded that LTE~model atmospheres can be used to analyse the available X-ray spectra of classical novae during their SSS~state.
elements.sent-2411-049: In this work we analyse the high-resolution~XMM and \chandra~spectra of RXJ0513 using the LTE~atmosphere grid calculated by \citet{Suleimanov_etal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Our aim is to test the contraction model of the source's variability by tracking the evolution of the WD~parameters and comparing them with the optical brightness of the source.
elements.sent-2411-051: The X-ray observations used are described in Sect.~\ref{sect:Observations .}
elements.sent-2411-052: We describe the used models atmospheres and the fitting technique in Sect.~\ref{sect:Method .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The results of spectral fitting are presented in Sect.~\ref{sect:Results , while in Sect.~\ref{sect:Discussion we discuss the possible nature of the obtained evolutionary trends.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: We conclude and summarise our findings in Sect.~\ref{sect:Conclusions .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The source was observed by the Chandra X-ray observatory using the High Resolution Camera~(HRC-S) and the Low Energy Transmission Grating~(LETG).
elements.sent-2411-056: A total of six spectra are publicly available in the Chandra Grating-Data Archive and Catalog\footnote{\url{http://tgcat.mit.edu ~\citep[TGCat, ][]{Huenemoerder_etal2011 , see Tab.~\ref{tab:log_chandra for the {\it Chandra observation log.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-058: To increase the signal-to-noise ratio the positive and negative first-order spectra were co-added using the combine\_grating\_spectra task of the Chandra Interactive Analysis of Observations~\citep[CIAO,][]{Fruscione_etal2006, CIAO_ascl package.}
elements.sent-2411-059: For the fitting purposes the spectra were rebinned to at least $30$~counts per bin in the $0.21-0.65\,\rm keV$~energy range.
elements.sent-2411-060: The high-resolution spectra, obtained using the Reflection Grating Spectrometer~\citep[RGS,][]{denHerder_etal2001 instrument, are available for eight observations, see Tab.~\ref{tab:log_xmm .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Note that the observation with ObsID~$0151410101$ was affected by high solar flaring activity and contains no useful data.
elements.sent-2411-062: All spectra are available in the {\it XMM-Newton Science Archive\footnote{\url{http://nxsa.esac.esa.int/nxsa-web , only the first-order spectrum was used without any rebinning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We did not combine the data from~RGS1 and~RGS2 but fitted them simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-064: Below we will refer to these spectra as $\rm X1-X8$.

elements.sent-2411-065: A new grid of hot LTE~model atmospheres, recently presented by \citet{Suleimanov_etal2024 , was used to analyse the observed spectra.}
elements.sent-2411-066: These model spectra were successfully used for the analysis of the classical SSS~CAL~$83$ and one of the Chandra spectra of RXJ0513~(spectrum~C3).
elements.sent-2411-067: The models were computed using a code based on Kurucz's atlas~\citep{Kurucz1970 , which was modified for high temperatures and high densities \citep{Ibragimov_etal2003, Suleimanov_etal2006, Suleimanov_etal2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The models account for the$15$~most abundant chemical elements from~\element{H to~\element{Ni and about $20,000$~lines from CHIANTI, Version~3.0, atomic database~\citep{Dere_etal1997 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The main parameters of the model are the effective temperature $\Teff$ and the gravity parameter $\dlg = \log\varg - \log\varg_{\rm Edd}$, which indicates the distance of model from the Eddington limit: \begin{equation}\log\varg_{\rm Edd} = \log(\sigma_{\rm e}\sigma_{\rm SB}T^4 /c) = 4.818 + 4\log(\Teff/10^5 \rm K),\end{equation} see details in \citet{Suleimanov_etal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: The grid is computed for~$\Teff$ in the range of~$100-1000\rm\, kK$ in steps of~$25\,\rm kK$.
elements.sent-2411-072: The grid is calculated for three chemical compositions; we used the LMC~composition, where the hydrogen-helium mix is set to solar, while the abundance of heavy elements is set to half-solar~$(A = 0.5)$.
elements.sent-2411-073: The model grid is available\footnote{\url{https://github.com/HEASARC/xspec_localmodels/tree/master/sss_atm as an XSPEC\footnote{\url{https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/docs/xanadu/xspec/ ~\citep{Arnaud1996, XSPEC_ascl table.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: All the observed X-ray spectra were fitted using the models described above.
elements.sent-2411-075: We used the WD~mass~$M$ and radius~$R$ as free parameters instead of~$\dlg$ and normalisation parameter.
elements.sent-2411-076: A uniform prior distribution was set for $\Teff$, $M$ (in range $0.3-1.4\,\rm M_{\sun}$), and $R$ (in range $(2-20) \times 10^8\,\rm cm$).
elements.sent-2411-077: Note that the strict upper limit was set for the WD~mass.
elements.sent-2411-078: Another limitation applied is based on the fact that the WD~radius must be greater than the cold WD~radius~$R_{\rm cold}$ at such a mass~\citep{Nauenberg1972 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: The Tübingen-Boulder ISM model tbabs \citep{Wilms2000 was employed to account for absorption, with a uniform prior distribution set for the hydrogen column density $\nh$~in the range of $(1-10)\times 10^{20 \,\rm cm^{-2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: To find the best-fitting parameters and estimate their uncertainties, we used the following technique.
elements.sent-2411-081: First, we employed the Bayesian approach.
elements.sent-2411-082: The analysis was conducted using the software Bayesian X-ray Analysis~\citep[BXA,][]{Buchner_etal2014, BXA_ascl , which connects the nested sampling package UltraNest\footnote{\url{https://johannesbuchner.github.io/UltraNest/ ~\citep[][]{Buchner2021, UltraNest_ascl with~xspec\,\footnote{\url{https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/docs/xanadu/xspec/ }\citep{Arnaud1996, XSPEC_ascl .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The nested sampling Monte Carlo algorithm MLFriends~\citep{Buchner_etal2014, Buchner2019 was used to obtain posterior probability distributions and the Bayesian evidence.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The usage of C-statistics~\citep{Cash1979 is preferable instead of the $\chi^2$-statistics for low-count spectra.}
elements.sent-2411-085: We used its~xspec implementation~cstat as a likelihood in order to determine the best-fit parameters.
elements.sent-2411-086: The parameter uncertainties are derived from the~$0.16$~and~$0.84$~quantiles of the posterior distribution (which corresponds to $68\%$ confidence level, CL).
elements.sent-2411-087: However, we consider these statistical errors to be significantly underestimated.
elements.sent-2411-088: To obtain more realistic uncertainties, we added a systematic error to the spectra such that the reduced~$\chi^2_\nu$ approached unity, after which we re-estimated the parameter confidence intervals.
elements.sent-2411-089: We then combined the statistical confidence intervals (derived using~cstat) with the systematic ones (calculated using~$\chi^2$).
elements.sent-2411-090: The final parameter value was taken as the mean of the combined confidence interval.
elements.sent-2411-091: Due to such technique of estimating the model parameters and its confidence intervals in the tables below we do not provide the statistics value or the goodness-of-fit estimating criterion~(like $\chi^2_{\nu}$).
elements.sent-2411-092: First of all, we fitted the observed spectra using the interstellar absorption column density $\nh$ as a free parameter.
elements.sent-2411-093: It was found that the obtained absorption parameter differs significantly across the various spectra.
elements.sent-2411-094: For the XMM~spectra, $\nh$ is contained within the range $(1-3.5)\times 10^{20}\,\rm cm^{-2}$, while for the \chandra~spectra $\nh$ lies within $(3.7-5.4)\times 10^{20}\,\rm cm^{-2}$.
elements.sent-2411-095: All obtained values are lower than the average Galactic absorption column in the direction of the source, which is $(6.3\pm 0.8) \times 10^{20}\,\rm cm^{-2}$ based on the extinction/absorption maps presented in \citet{Doroshenko2024}\footnote{\url{http://astro.uni-tuebingen.de/nh3d/nhtool .}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Note that the fitting parameters are crucially dependend on the obtained absorption parameter~$\nh$~\citep[see e.g.][]{Suleimanov_etal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-097: Consequently, we adopted $\nh=(5.5\pm1)\times 10^{20}\,\rm cm^{-2}$, as obtained by \citet{Gaensicke_etal1998 from the analysis of the UV~spectra of the source, and will use this value further.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Note that this issue also arises when fitting soft X-ray spectra of other~SSSs with atmosphere models~\citep[see e.g.][]{Rauch_etal2010, Suleimanov_etal2024, Tavleev_etal2024 .}

elements.sent-2411-149: The observed anti-correlation between optical and soft X-ray fluxes in~RXJ0513 is usually explained by the so-called contraction model~\citep[e.g.][]{Reinsch_etal1996, Reinsch_etal2000, Southwell_etal1996, Hachisu_etal2003a, Hachisu_etal2003b .}
elements.sent-2411-150: According to this model, the mass accretion rate~$\dot{M}$ is relatively low during the X-ray bright state, and the nuclear burning rate in the envelope is close to the steady-burning boundary.
elements.sent-2411-151: This state of the source corresponds to an almost minimal radius of the~WD~photosphere, with the highest effective temperature and a relatively low bolometric luminosity.
elements.sent-2411-152: When~$\dot{M}$ increases, the~WD inflates and the bolometric luminosity slightly increases, while the effective temperature drops.
elements.sent-2411-153: The decrease in effective temperature suggests that the peak of the spectral energy distribution shifts towards lower photon energies, and a smaller fraction of the emitted spectrum falls within the observed soft X-ray band.
elements.sent-2411-154: Therefore, the increase in the WD~photosphere radius at an almost constant bolometric luminosity leads to a decrease in the observed soft X-ray flux.
elements.sent-2411-155: Thus, within the framework of the contraction model, the low X-ray flux corresponds to a high mass accretion rate, a large WD~radius and a high bolometric luminosity.
elements.sent-2411-156: Conversely, the bright X-ray state is associated with a hotter~WD with a relatively small photospheric radius and a lower bolometric luminosity.
elements.sent-2411-157: In the considered model the optical flux is produced by accretion disc illumination and reprocessing of hard radiation into the optical band.
elements.sent-2411-158: A colder~WD with an expanded photosphere illuminates the accretion disc more significantly, meaning that the faint X-ray state should correspond to a high optical state.
elements.sent-2411-159: Conversely, a hotter~WD with a deflated photosphere illuminates the accretion disc less effectively, so the low optical state corresponds to the bright X-ray state, leading to the observed anti-correlation between optical and soft X-ray fluxes from the source.
elements.sent-2411-160: However, our results contradict this model.
elements.sent-2411-161: We show in Figs.~\ref{fig:pars_OPflux_xmm ~and~\ref{fig:pars_OPflux_chandra that the bright optical state of~RXJ0513 corresponds to a low X-ray luminosity, during which the source lies below the stable-burning strip on the $\Teff - \log\varg$ plane, see Fig.~\ref{fig:glogT .}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: The found contradiction could be explained if we take into account the effects of thermalization of hard radiation and its re-emission in the optical part of the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-163: The disc photosphere itself transforms the external soft X-ray radiation into the optical band very inefficiently.
elements.sent-2411-164: The reprocessing efficiency for soft X-rays and far~UV radiation in irradiated accretion disc photospheres in~SSSs is low~\citep[$\eta\sim 0.05-0.1,\, F_{\rm opt =\eta\, F_{\rm soft\, X-ray}$, see e.g.][]{Suleimanov_etal1999 .}}
elements.sent-2411-165: The opacity of the upper atmospheric layers in soft X-ray is so high that the irradiated flux is absorbed before reaching the optical flux formation depth.
elements.sent-2411-166: The absorbed flux is then re-radiated mainly in far UV~spectral lines.
elements.sent-2411-167: Reprocessing efficiency can increase to acceptable values of~$\eta\sim 0.3-0.5$ if we assume a system of optically thick~(in soft X-ray) clouds above the disc.
elements.sent-2411-168: In this case, the total reprocessing efficiency rises due to multiple scattering of soft X-ray and far~UV radiation between clouds~\citep{Suleimanov_etal2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-169: Figure~\ref{fig:model_image presents a qualitative illustration of the proposed model.}
elements.sent-2411-170: The value of~$\eta$ depends on the effective optical depth of the cloud slab~\citep{Suleimanov_etal2003 , \begin{equation}\tau_{\rm eff} \approx \pi R_{\rm cl}^2\,N_{\rm cl} L,\end{equation} where $R_{\rm cl}$ is the cloud size, $N_{\rm cl}$ is the cloud number density, and $L$~is the geometrical thickness of the cloud slab.}
elements.sent-2411-171: The reprocessing efficiency reaches the maximum at~$\tau_{\rm eff \approx 1-10$.}
elements.sent-2411-172: The nature of these hypothetical clouds is not known.
elements.sent-2411-173: However, clouds formed due to the thermal instability of the matter illuminated by photoionizing radiation are more likely~\citep[see, e.g.][]{2002ApJ...581.1297J .}
elements.sent-2411-174: Thus, the changes in the optical flux of~RXJ0513 could be explained by assuming that thermal instability conditions depend on the state of the WD~photosphere.
elements.sent-2411-175: Namely, a relatively small and low luminous WD~photosphere provides better conditions for cloud formation.
elements.sent-2411-176: Consequently, the effective optical thickness of the cloud slab increases, and we observe the bright optical state of the source, accompanied by the faint X-ray flux.
elements.sent-2411-177: Moreover, the cloud slab might even become so geometrically thick that it obscures the entire X-ray source.
elements.sent-2411-178: And vice versa, a luminous~WD with an expanded photosphere evaporates the clouds, reducing~$\tau_{\rm eff}$, and thereby decreasing reprocessing efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-179: Both of the hypothetical states are illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:model_image .}

elements.sent-2411-180: In this work, we performed a spectral analysis of the supersoft X-ray source RX~J$0513.9-6951$, which was observed in X-rays by the \chandra~and XMM-Newton telescopes during its optically low states, when the source exhibits maximum X-ray brightness.
elements.sent-2411-181: To describe the spectra we used a grid of the high-gravity hot LTE~model atmospheres, calculated by~\citet{Suleimanov_etal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-183: We adopted the LMC~composition, with the hydrogen-helium mix set to solar and the abundance of heavy elements set to half-solar~$(A = 0.5)$.
elements.sent-2411-184: We performed two joint fits of the XMM~and~\chandra~spectra with different values of a common WD~mass parameter and then compared the results with theoretical $\Teff - \log\varg$ and $\Teff - L$ model curves.
elements.sent-2411-185: The fit positions correspond to $M=1.1\pm 0.1\,M_\sun$, which we adopted as the obtained WD~mass.
elements.sent-2411-186: Our analysis revealed the evolution of the~WD in RXJ0513: the photospheric radius of the~WD and its bolometric luminosity increase as the optical flux decreases, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-187: This conclusion is based on studying the source's position on the~$\Teff- \log\varg$ plane, where the source lies below the stable-burning strip when it is optically bright.
elements.sent-2411-188: As its optical brightness decreases, RXJ0513 shifts towards the stable-burning strip.
elements.sent-2411-189: We also found the correlation between the photospheric WD~radius and the source magnitude in R-band, as well as between the bolometric luminosity and R-band magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-190: These results contradict the predictions of the contraction model, which is commonly used to explain the observed anti-correlation between soft X-ray and optical fluxes~\citep[see, e.g.][]{Reinsch_etal1996, Reinsch_etal2000, Southwell_etal1996 .}
elements.sent-2411-191: This model predicts the opposite correlation between the WD~photospheric radius and its optical brightness.
elements.sent-2411-192: We propose an alternative model of~RXJ0513 periodicity based on the reprocessing model suggested by \citet{Suleimanov_etal2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: In this model, efficient reprocessing of soft X-ray flux illuminating the accretion disc is achieved through multiple scattering of hard radiation in a system of optically thick~(in the soft X-ray band) gaseous blobs/clouds above the accretion disc, embedded in a disc wind or corona.
elements.sent-2411-194: These clouds may arise due to thermal instability in the matter above the disc, irradiated by far UV/soft X-ray flux.
elements.sent-2411-195: We suggest that the cloud system becomes highly saturated when the~WD has relatively low luminosity and a small radius, comparable to that of a cold~WD.
elements.sent-2411-196: In this case, a cloud slab provides reprocessing efficiency high enough to transform the soft X-ray/far UV flux into the optical band.
elements.sent-2411-197: This picture is proposed to represent the~RXJ0513 state with low X-ray flux and high optical brightness.
elements.sent-2411-198: Alternatively, a luminous~WD with an expanded photosphere is likely less favourable for clouds formation.
elements.sent-2411-199: The cloud slab becomes rarefied, and the reprocessing efficiency decreases.
elements.sent-2411-200: This picture corresponds to the source's low optical state, accompanied by high X-ray flux.
elements.sent-2411-201: This suggested toy model explains qualitatively the observed properties of~RXJ0513 and may open a window for observational investigations of thermal instability in plasma irradiated by hard UV/soft X-ray flux.
elements.sent-2411-202: This behaviour is believed to be the result of the periodic expansion and contraction of the~WD due to variations in the accretion rate in the system.
elements.sent-2411-203: This means that the optical brightness of the system is lower when the~WD is larger, more luminous, and illuminates the accretion disc more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-204: We use a model that assumes that the far UV/soft X-ray flux is reprocessed into the optical band due to multiple scattering in the cloud system above the accretion disc.

elements.sent-2411-255: In this work, we performed a spectral analysis of the supersoft X-ray source RX~J$0513.9-6951$, which was observed in X-rays by the \chandra~and \xmm~telescopes during its optically low states, when the source exhibits maximum X-ray brightness.
elements.sent-2411-256: We performed two joint fits of the \xmm~and~\chandra~spectra with different values of a common WD~mass parameter and then compared the results with theoretical $\Teff - \log\varg$ and $\Teff - L$ model curves.
elements.sent-2411-257: The fit positions correspond to $M=1.1\pm 0.1\,M_\sun$, which we adopt as the obtained WD~mass.
elements.sent-2411-258: Our analysis reveal the evolution of the~WD in RXJ0513: the photospheric radius of the~WD and its bolometric luminosity increase as the optical flux decreases, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-259: We also find the correlation between the photospheric WD~radius and the source magnitude in R-band, as well as between the bolometric luminosity and R-band magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-260: These results contradict the predictions of the contraction model, which is commonly used to explain the observed anti-correlation between soft X-ray and optical fluxes~\citep[see e.g.][]{Reinsch_etal1996, Reinsch_etal2000, Southwell_etal1996 .}
elements.sent-2411-261: This suggested toy model qualitatively explains the observed properties of~RXJ0513 and may open a window for observational investigations of thermal instability in plasma irradiated by hard UV/soft X-ray flux.
